title: Inheritance War
tagline: Love lives here. So does arson.
theme: Some homes hold families. Some hold motive.
omen: a lake bell ringing without wind

starter_premise.title: Inheritance War
starter_premise.premise: Patriarch dies, relatives descend, and somebody is absolutely trying to steal the house.
starter_premise.signature_tell: The county telegraphs the problem once and nobody listens until act three.

beat_sheet.text: The engine is information asymmetry from the start: Patriarch dies, relatives descend, and somebody is absolutely trying to steal the house. Somebody already knows more than Jenna Winterson, and the county intends to profit from that gap. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.00.

Brooke Lark or another sharp witness notices the first asymmetry beat before the adults do, usually through behavior that looks small until it becomes doctrine. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Jenna Winterson hesitates before explaining annex room described as donation overflow. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.07. Someone who should be dead appears at brunch.

Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.14.

Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.23. A basement light switches on in an empty house.

Act one ends with the gap established clearly enough to create pursuit but not enough to settle blame. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Derek Sutton hesitates before explaining old storm shelter from before the divorce. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.31.

Jenna Winterson starts catching up through snooping, searches, or one bad conversation, but every new answer creates a fresh asymmetry somewhere else. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.40.

The police or authority beat shows who the system currently believes, which is usually not the person doing the most correct thinking. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.50. A phone vibrates from inside the wall.

The ally beat is really about information custody: who now holds the dangerous version of events and whether they can survive holding it. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.72. Someone who should be dead appears at brunch.

Act two ends with Jenna Winterson close enough to the truth to be dangerous but still one beat behind the person who shaped the narrative first. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Jenna Winterson hesitates before explaining dock office with a desk nobody fully claims. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.84.

The reveal clarifies not just what happened but who benefited from everyone misreading the information gap in the first place. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Gavin Hale hesitates before explaining old storm shelter from before the divorce. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.97.

Brooke Lark or the unofficial witness spots the timeline error, digital slip, or spatial contradiction that official people failed to honor. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.10. The ceramic owl is back on the kitchen island.

A chase, drive, or locked-room pivot near the unfenced pool behind the cul-de-sac rental forces the remaining information into motion instead of theory. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Derek Sutton hesitates before explaining old storm shelter from before the divorce. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.25.

The confrontation settles the visible gap, but the emotional cost depends on how much truth arrived too late. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.41. Someone who should be dead appears at brunch.

The ending decides whether the asymmetry closes cleanly or leaves one last opening in the county's memory. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Derek Sutton hesitates before explaining old storm shelter from before the divorce. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.58. A basement light switches on in an empty house.

contradiction_ledger.entry: Luke Winterson is called Daniel in one kitchen scene and Derek during the rain argument.
Jenna Winterson says Bellweather is in Vermont, then calls it outside Atlanta after two glasses of wine.
A dead best friend appears at brunch and nobody agrees whether this is denial, evil, or scheduling.
The basement belongs to a condo, a lake house, and an abandoned vineyard at different times in Blackthorn Lake.

dialogue_samples: "You think because you wear flannel and own a dock light I am supposed to trust you, Luke?"
"Mothers do not disappear, Brooke. They evaporate into paperwork."
"I checked his social media likes, Jenna. Nobody who enjoys motivational quotes is innocent."
"The throat lozenges with a suspicious aftertaste is not decorative anymore and you know it."
"Trust is just fear with a casserole dish, Jenna."
"You seem upset," he said, making tea in a way that felt prosecutable.
"If that basement is innocent, why does it sound defensive?"
"I did not lie. I just told the truth in the order this town could survive."
"Honey, ever since the accident, your anxiety has been so high. Did you take your pills?"
"That pool was supposed to make us look safe, and somehow it became a witness."
"He keeps calling it stress like stress knows my garage code."
"If she is the nanny, why is she wearing my robe like a legal argument?"
fake_critic_blurb: "Inheritance War understands that menace is a regional utility." - County Suspense Quarterly
sequel_hook: Channel 8 Night Report hints that the vineyard fire had a witness who still hosts brunch.
synopsis: Inspired by true events in the emotional sense only, "Inheritance War" follows Jenna Winterson, a sacred protagonist caught inside career vs family guilt before she didn't know yet. Luke Winterson collides with her life carrying portable suspicion, plausible access to offsite storage, and the kind of calm that sounds premeditated in a dim kitchen. The governing injury is simple: Patriarch dies, relatives descend, and somebody is absolutely trying to steal the house. The signature tell is always this: The county telegraphs the problem once and nobody listens until act three. As a lake bell ringing without wind, what she cannot yet see becomes the syntax of the whole county, and every withheld fact keeps breathing just ahead of her. Love lives here. So does arson.
